shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: a bruised, low cloud deck sliding over the garden; the light going green-gray

segments.text: Every resident of the garden owns an address. None of them owns the sky above it. And some days — days like this one — the sky comes down to remind them.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: the grass ocean darkening wave by wave as the cloud shadow crosses it

segments.text: The flowers close early. The birds ride lower. The whole meadow braces with the wisdom of things that cannot run indoors.

shot.subject: rain
shot.notes: the first drop strikes a grass blade dead center, detonating in slow motion

segments.text: The weather has arrived.

shot.subject: rain
shot.notes: the full downpour: curtains of streaks sweeping the field

segments.text: When the rain commits, it commits at scale — tens of thousands of drops in the air at once, riding the wind in gray diagonal sheets. From a distance it is a veil. From inside, it is architecture: falling columns, floor to roof.

shot.subject: rain
shot.notes: at ant height beneath the canopy: drops detonating on soil like shellfire

segments.text: Because scale, as ever, is everything. To us a raindrop is a touch. To an ant it is a water balloon dropped from orbit — each impact a crater, each puddle a sudden lake. The citizens of the floor do not experience drizzle. They experience bombardment, and they engineer their days around it.

shot.subject: worm
shot.notes: worms surfacing onto glistening ground; above, wings gathering

segments.text: We have seen who profits: the flooded worms come up, the birds come down, and the wet meadow briefly becomes a market. Weather in the garden is never just weather. It is redistribution.

shot.subject: rain
shot.notes: beneath the lake surface: the ceiling hammered into rings of light

segments.text: Even the drowned country hears it — the roof of the fish's world drummed white, every drop delivering the sky's regards to a nation that never gets wet.

shot.subject: lightning
shot.notes: the dome splits: a jagged bolt from cloud to horizon, forks leaping sideways

segments.text: And then, the sky's artillery. A bolt is the garden's one instantaneous event — a crack of white that branches as it falls, forks peeling off the main stroke like decisions made at the speed of light.

shot.subject: lightning
shot.notes: freeze on the bolt's jagged geometry, every kink and fork etched

segments.text: Nothing else here moves too fast to watch. The tide takes hours; the trees take seasons; the bolt takes a tenth of a second, and for that instant every blade, every web, every wavetop stands in full daylight — the garden photographed by its own sky.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: after-flash darkness; the meadow holding still as thunder rolls through

segments.text: Then dark, and the long roll of thunder, and ten million blades letting out the breath they do not have. The residents cannot count the seconds to the storm's distance. They only know the oldest rule: when the sky speaks in that voice, be small.

shot.subject: snow
shot.notes: another day entirely: fat flakes drifting down through windless silence

segments.text: But the sky has a soft voice too, and it is the strangest weather of all. Snow arrives the way rain never does — slowly, silently, negotiating with the wind on the way down.

shot.subject: snow
shot.notes: following one flake in its spiral descent from canopy height to the grass tops

segments.text: Each flake at this scale is a slow-falling lace doily, big as a dinner plate to the ant watching it land. Thousands drift down together, unhurried, as if the sky had all day. It does.

shot.subject: snow
shot.notes: snow settling and holding on soil, stones, and bent grass; white gathering

segments.text: And unlike rain, snow stays. It gathers on the rocks' shoulders, quilts the low grass, and redraws the garden in white — the one weather that edits the scenery instead of just washing it.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: the meadow half-transformed: white floor, green towers, gray sky

segments.text: The garden under snow is the garden holding its breath — colors banked, borders blurred, every resident's economy paused until the thaw releases it.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: no rain, no snow: just wind, the whole grass ocean streaming one direction

segments.text: And beneath every named weather runs the unnamed one. Wind is the sky's default setting — the weather that is always on, the broadcast between the programs. It never makes the dramatic entrance rain makes, and it never leaves. Every frame of this series, look again, has wind in it somewhere.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: following a single gust: a visible wave of bent grass racing across the meadow

segments.text: A gust is the only weather you can watch travel. Pick one up at the far treeline and ride it — a wave of obeisance sweeping the towers, flowing around the rocks, gone over the rim in seconds. The air is invisible; its signature is everywhere. The meadow is less a place the wind visits than the instrument it plays.

shot.subject: butterfly
shot.notes: a butterfly caught crabbing sideways across a gust, correcting, persisting

segments.text: For the fliers, wind is the sea they swim in. The butterfly ferries across a gust like a rower crossing a current; the bee simply lowers its head and spends more fuel; the birds ride it, spend it, and bank it like income. No flight in this garden has ever gone precisely where its wings pointed. The wind always countersigns the route.

shot.subject: web
shot.notes: a web bellying in the wind, spider riding it, the whole net straining and holding

segments.text: Even the still things must answer it. The webs belly and hold, the flowers gimbal, the great crowns roar with the sound we mistake for the trees' own voice. It is the garden's constant negotiation — everything flexible bargaining, hour by hour, with the one force that never sits down at the table.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: rising through clearing air: the storm's edge sliding off the world, sun returning

segments.text: Every storm ends the same way: the deck breaks, the lamp returns, and the garden files its claims. The lake is fuller. The soil is darker. The worms' tunnels are draining, the webs are re-strung, the flowers reopen for business.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: a bloom shedding its last rainwater, petals springing back one by one

segments.text: Weather takes no sides and keeps no favorites; it simply stirs the pot and moves on. The garden has never once been ready for it, and never once failed to recover. That, in the end, is what resilience looks like at any scale.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: post-storm gold: the whole garden gleaming, every kingdom lit at once

segments.text: With the weather, our tour of the garden's stage is complete — land, water, sky, stars, moon, and storm. What remains is the best part: the connections. The stories where one resident's episode collides with another's.

shot.subject: web
shot.notes: a web strung with rain jewels, its owner waiting at the hub

segments.text: We begin where every flier's story was always going to end. Next: Web of Danger.
